texts.full_text: and maintenance, grafting techniques and management of grafted seedlings. Access to improved seeds and planting materials is a major constraint for fruit and vegetable producers in the district, but after Ejigu was trained in grafted avocado seedlings production and management, he planted eight demonstration avocado mother trees provided by LIVES. He also actively experiments with integrated nursery management practIces in his farmland. He produced about 750 grafted avocado seedlings and earned ETB 25,000 (USD 1,200) from selling more than 500 seedlings to relatives, the Kersa Office of Agriculture and other producers in his PA and beyond. Ejigu now coaches other farmers who have bought grafted avocado seedlings, and says there is high demand for grafted fruit tree seedlings, particularly for apple mango trees. LIVES in collaboration with the Irrigation Development Office of Kersa District held a field day at Ejigu’s farm to promote and create demand for improved fruit and vegetable production practices following which the Kersa District Administration promoted his work in the presence of PA administrations in the district. The district’s natural resources department of the Office of Agriculture later invited him to train producers and development agents in six farmer training centres, on avocado grafted seedling production and management. He later carried out follow up coaching of the participants. According to Ejigu, coaching after training helps farmers better apply their new knowledge and skills. Ejigu is now a key local resource person who trains and coaches producers and Development Agents (DAs) on seeds, seedling production and grafting techniques. Saying that he has learnt much from the LIVES project, Ejigu adds that ‘agriculture today is not like in our fathers’ time,’. He encourages farmers to learn new skills and diversify into fruit and vegetable farming. He has a plan to buy a high-power motor pump and to dig a shallow well to expand his irrigated fodder cultivation of desho, napier grass and alfalfa, for his planned dairy business. texts.full_text: findings in Sera were analyzed from patients who were suspected to have antibodies to neutrophils. The analysis comprised five methods which avoid heterologous antibodies to human immunoglobulin. These methods were the granulocyte agglutination test (GAT), the granulocyte cytotoxicity test (GCT), the monocyte cytotoxicity test (MCT), the lymphocyte cytotoxicity test (LCT) and immune phagocytosis inhibition test (IPI). Each serum was tested with cells from five donors, at least, and some with cells from relatives. After exclusion of sera containing multiple antibodies and HLA-antibodies with positive LCT- and IPI-tests, the GAT- or GCT-reactive antibodies were significantly detected in 65% cases using these methods [ref]. These included both specific immunoglobulin heavy chain light chains as well non–antibody dependent cellular cytotoxicity mechanisms such asspecific granzyme B and perforin defects, highlighting the complexity of diagnosing this condition. The implications for patients with antibodies in immune neutropenias are significant; they can lead to severe infections due inability or reduced abilityof their body's defense system (neutrophils) fight off pathogens effectively [ref]. This enhances collaborative performance and creates a positive team environment. 6. Overcoming Psychological Barriers: Fear of failure, anxiety, or lack of confidence can hinder performance. Life Skill Development: The principles of sports psychology, such as discipline, goal-setting, and stress management, extend beyond sports. Let the photographer handle the smiles At the session, it's time to let the photographer take charge. That means if a grandchild isn't smiling or a sibling is feeling rebellious, don't scold or reprimand. Kids need time to warm up to the idea of having their portrait taken, so it's okay if they aren't cooperative at first. We have lots of tricks we can use to get smiles. The main takeaway here is that people smile naturally when they are having a good time, so we do our best to make the session a fun experience. Oh, and please, don't say cheese. texts.full_text: up by an understanding of how another person feels. “They never saw that little house again” is a painful little sentence; wistful. Laura does not think there are too many people, but she loves someone who does. And so she learns to see the doe and the fawn, and the bear, eating berries in a patch on sunlight. She looks through her father’s eyes. I think that is what happens in a family, and that is what we mean with the word “home” – our identities get formed as they are mixed up a bit. We become practiced in shifting perspective. The idea of living in harmony with wildness becomes valued, even by someone who might prefer human company. We share in the making of each other. In his essay The House Beautiful, the Unitarian minister William Channing Gannett wrote that it is really light that turns a house into a home, but that true light comes from within and is expressed through our eyes and smiles and little habits that are like caresses. This kind of illumination warms the whole world, by putting us outside ourselves. Radiance is felt, he says, like a “low pervading music that we cannot hear. It is cheer, it is peace, it is trust, it is delight – it is all these things. It is love-light.” This is what causes the glow around the word “home.” Of course not everyone has happy memories of home, and the word may not conjure anything good at all. There are hurts that never do mend, and some dreams dashed like so much broken pottery. But I think that is part of the allure – that home is such a mixture of pain and longing and idealism. Even if we have never experienced it, we know what we mean and what we are pining for when we want to go home. We want comfort, acceptance, understanding. We want belonging. And we want to just be. We don’t want to have to make a new place less strange and foreign.

She shares her birthday with Jarvis Cocker and David Seaman andlike Jarvis Cocker she wears glasses but has nothing whatsoever in common with David Seaman. She is fascinatedby words, people and their stories, and regularly spends hours fantasisingabout being offered a weekly column. She has a degree in Filmand Television which she gained from Brunel Universityin 1997. She has been blessed with twoquite remarkablechildrenwhom she adores.She enjoys the company of herfriends and the circus thatislife. Long Walk to Forever by Kurt Vonnegut is her favourite short story. She is the author ofthe published children's tale The StoryofBalan Singh, and is currently working on herfirstbook. texts.full_text: The present invention relates to a random number generating device that generates a random number by the behavior of a microcomputer. When electrically-powered equipment (electric power tools, electrically operated machines, and so on), which operate in response to power supply from batteries, and battery chargers, which charge batteries, are used, there is a need for a reliable and efficient method to ensure that the operations and functions of these devices remain unpredictable for certain security or testing purposes, such as preventing unauthorized access or creating secure testing environments. The random number generating device, which utilizes the inherent variability and unpredictable behavior of a microcomputer, can serve this need effectively. This device can generate true random numbers by leveraging the physical phenomena and timing variations that occur within the microcomputer's operations, such as variations in clock jitter, thermal noise, or other physical processes that are difficult to predict and model. By integrating such a random number generator into the control circuitry of electrically-powered equipment or battery chargers, it can enhance the security and robustness

texts.full_text: there were moments where the storytelling felt rushed, as if Taibbi was trying to pack in too many stories or experiences. However, these minor critiques do not diminish the overall impact of the book. Taibbi's insights into the various derangements he encounters—be it political corruption, religious fanaticism, or economic greed—are sharp and unflinching. His ability to weave humor with profound observations makes for a captivating read that is both enlightening and entertaining. The Great Derangement not only exposes the absurdities of our world but also offers a critical perspective on the decline of the American Empire through its lens. One particularly impressive aspect of Taibbi's work is his willingness to delve into areas where few others dare to tread, whether it be infiltrating extremist groups or navigating complex geopolitical landscapes. His bravery and journalistic integrity are commendable, as he does not shy away from challenging narratives that many would prefer left unexamined. In conclusion, while there are a few elements in The Great Derangement that could have been improved upon, the book remains a powerful testament to taibbi's unique voice and his commitment to exposing the truth. It's a must-read for anyone interested in contemporary political commentary, social critique, or just plain old good

texts.full_text: she joined the New England Emigrant Aid Society and soon moved her family to a claim in southern Douglas County, near Prairie City. Clarina Nichols’ contribution to reform in Kansas focused mostly on woman’s rights, but like most activist women of her day, she also was opposed to slavery. Her husband died in 1855, and Nichols spent much of 1856 on the campaign trail seeking support for Kansas and John C. Fremont, Republican presidential nominee. on October 4, 1856, she wrote Thaddeus Hyatt regarding the objectives of her recent speaking tour in Pennsylvania: “In the first place I am laboring . . . to subsist my two sons in the Free State army of Kansas by the pay I get from the lectures.” Subsequently, Nichols moved the family to Wyandotte County, where in the spring of 1857 she became associate editor of the Quindaro Chindowan, an antislavery newspaper. Nichols traveled throughout the territory lecturing about equality, gathering signatures on petitions, and by 1859 building support for her participation at the Wyandotte Constitutional Convention. These petitions persuaded the delegates to give Nichols a voice and a platform. She sat in on the convention’s daily proceedings, occupying a seat of prominence next to the chaplain. While there, she lobbied the delegates to grant women equal educational opportunities and the right to vote in school district elections, as well as equal standing on child custody matters and equality in holdi

We consider two self-propelled spheres (microswimmers) moving towards each other in viscous fluid. We first show that under commonly used no-slip boundary conditions on the fluid-solid interface the microswimmers do not collide which is a generalization of the well-known no-collision paradox for solid bodies (with no self-propulsion) in a viscous fluid. Secondly, we show that the microswimmers do collide when the no-slip boundary conditions are replaced by the Navier boundary conditions which therefore provides an adequate model of microswimmers such as swimming bacteria. The self-propulsion mechanism generates a drag force pulling a bacterium backwards and the collision problem is reduced to the analysis of competition between the drag and self-propulsion. All patients had an increased pulmonary FDG uptake (median SUVmax=3.1 [2-7.6]), with a median extent of 19% [6-67]. Consolidations, ground-glass opacities, honeycombing and reticulations showed uptake in 90%, 89%, 85% and 76%, respectively. FDG uptake extent was associated with improvement of pulmonary function under treatment (increase in forced vital capacity>10%, p=0.03), whereas SUVmax and HRCT fibrosis score were not (p>0.5). texts.full_text: enters, and investment centers. Each type of center has a specific role and responsibility within the organization. Cost Centers: A cost center is a department or unit within a company that generates expenses but does not directly contribute to revenue. The primary goal of a cost center is to manage and control costs, ensuring that the organization's resources are utilized efficiently. Examples of cost centers include human resources, accounting, and IT departments. Profit Centers: As previously mentioned, a profit center is a section of a company that is responsible for generating revenue and controlling costs to contribute to the organization's overall profitability. Examples of profit centers can be found in sales teams, product lines, and geographic regions where the company operates. Investment Centers: An investment center is a part of a company that not only generates revenue and controls costs but also manages assets and investments. Managers of investment centers are held accountable for the return on investment (ROI) and are responsible for making decisions that maximize the company's profitability and growth. Examples of investment centers include divisions or subsidiaries that operate with a degree of financial autonomy. Organizing a business into these different types of centers allows for better allocation of resources, clearer accountability, and more effective decision-making. It includes not only basic needs such as food, shelter, clothing but also education for children or medical care when needed without falling into debt due these essentials expenses. In recent years there has been a growing concern about income inequality in our society where many workers are struggling to make ends meet despite working full time jobs which is unacceptable. Living wage can help bridge this gap by ensuring that all citizens have the ability and opportunity for economic stability. Adding living wages discussion into council's agenda would be an important step towards addressing these issues within Hamilton City, promoting social equity among its residents while also boosting local economy as it encourages more spending power to circulate in our community. This will not only improve quality of life but can help attract new businesses and talent who value a fair working environment. Therefore I propose that the council consider adding this discussion into their agenda for future meetings so we may work towards creating an inclusive, equitable city where all citizens have access opportunities necessary to thrive. This proposal is open any feedback or suggestions from fellow members of our community. Thank you in advance your consideration and cooperation on such a critical matter affecting every citizen within Hamilton City Council

texts.full_text: Let $Y$ be a smooth complex projective Calabi-Yau threefold. Let $I_n(Y,\beta)$ be the moduli space of ideal sheaves $I_{Z}\subset {\mathcal O}_{Y}$, where the associated subscheme $Z$ has maximal dimension equal to one, the holomorphic Euler characteristic $\chi({\mathcal O}_Z)$ is equal to $n$, and the associated 1-cycle has class $\beta\in H_2(Y)$. Recall that $I_n(Y,\beta)$ has a natural symmetric obstruction theory [@RT], [@BF2]. Hence we have the (degree zero) virtual fundamental class of $I_n(Y,\beta)$, whose degree $N_n(Y,\beta)\in{{\mathbb Z}}$ is the associated Donaldson-Thomas invariant. ---

texts.full_text: TILL NO. 1 March 09, 1992|By TOM PETERS It's Kafkaesque. The shrill voices of Michael Crichton (author of the crude "Rising Sun") and presidential hopefuls Pat Buchanan and Bob Kerrey claim we're on the ropes. Raise high the walls around Fortress America. And all this just one year after the Persian Gulf war, three months after the Soviet Union disintegrated. The recession lingers. The K-12 educational system shows few signs of life. Our infrastructure crumbles. Etc. Still, our grocery store shelves groan with an average of 30,000 items, up from 9,000 in 1976. At death's door? Last year brought 64 new varieties of spaghetti sauce alone! And while gloom engulfs Detroit, the Big Three's cars are getting better. I'm as proud of Wal-Mart as I used to be of GM. That aging giant spits up bloody phlegm, but had one long, rewarding-for-us-all run on Broadway. Now we've got other hits -- far more dynamic and innovative companies leading the way in various sectors. Consider technology: Silicon Valley continues to drive innovation with groundbreaking advancements that shape industries worldwide. Companies like Apple, Google, Amazon, Microsoft, and countless startups are at the forefront of technological progress, creating products and services that redefine how we live and work every day. In healthcare, American research institutions lead global efforts in medical breakthroughs—from developing cutting-edge treatments for diseases to pioneering new technologies such as CRISPR gene editing. The U.S.'s biotech sector is a powerhouse driving forward the frontiers of science and medicine. The entertainment industry remains unmatched globally, with Hollywood producing films that captivate audiences worldwide while streaming platforms like Netflix continue expanding their reach across borders. American music continues its global influence through artists who dominate charts everywhere they go. Moreover, our higher education system attracts students from around the globe
